era_context: Classical Greece in the early consolidation phase following the Persian Wars. Athens is strengthening its position within the Delian League. Cimon is rising to prominence. Sparta remains the dominant land power but is increasingly concerned about Athenian expansion. In China, the Spring and Autumn Period is transitioning to the Warring States Period; regional kingdoms are consolidating power. In India, early Buddhist communities are organizing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Persia (limited Greek sources for internal Persian affairs)

events.title: Aeschylus' Persians performed at Athens
events.description: The Greek tragedian Aeschylus presents his play 'Persians' (Persai) at the City Dionysia festival in Athens. This is the only surviving Greek tragedy based on a contemporary historical event—the Persian Wars. The play dramatizes the Persian perspective on the Greek victory, particularly the Battle of Salamis, and was performed just eight years after the battle itself. The play won first prize at the festival. It provides valuable insight into how Greeks understood and commemorated the Persian Wars and reflects the cultural and political atmosphere of Athens in the early 470s BCE.
key_figures: Pericles (possibly the choregos, or wealthy patron, though this is debated)

sources.name: Argument to Aeschylus' Persians (ancient scholarly commentary)

events.certainty_note: The play Persians survives and is dated to 472 BCE by the ancient argument (didascalia) that accompanies it. This is one of the few events from this year that is precisely dated in surviving sources.
cross_references: Related to -480-001 (Battle of Salamis, subject of the play)
Part of broader cultural memory of Persian Wars

events.title: Consolidation of Delian League structure
events.description: The Delian League continues to consolidate its structure and operations. Member states are assessed for tribute or naval contributions. Athens is gradually increasing its control over league affairs. The league's treasury remains on Delos, but Athens is the de facto leader. The exact events of 472 BCE are not well-documented, but the general pattern of league consolidation is clear from later historical accounts.
key_figures: Aristides the Just (Athenian statesman, associated with league organization)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.96–97

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of league consolidation is well-attested, but specific events in 472 BCE are not documented. The year 472 BCE is not particularly significant in surviving sources.
cross_references: Part of broader -470s period of Athenian expansion

disconfirming_evidence: The date of Aeschylus' Persians is given as 472 BCE in the ancient argument, but some modern scholars have questioned this date, suggesting it might be 473 or 471 BCE. However, the ancient tradition is strong and widely accepted. No major disconfirming evidence exists for the events listed, but the year 472 BCE is generally poorly documented in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 472 BCE is sparse. The only well-documented event is Aeschylus' Persians, which survives as a text. For other regions and aspects of Greek history, 472 BCE is not specifically documented; events are often dated to the 'early 470s' without precision. Chinese and Indian sources do not provide specific information for this year. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward Athens and Greek cultural production.

graph_edges.note: Aeschylus' Persians commemorates the Battle of Salamis
Delian League consolidation leads to Athenian imperialism
